topic: Why Uranus Rotates On Its Side
title: The Giant Impact That Tipped Uranus Over

lesson.learn: Uranus has an axial tilt of about 98 degrees, which means it spins almost on its side. The leading explanation is that a large collision early in the planet's history knocked it over after it had already formed. Because of that tilt, Uranus experiences extreme seasons: each pole can spend decades in sunlight, then decades in darkness.
lesson.simpler: Uranus got hit hard early on, and the impact tipped the planet onto its side. That is why its seasons last so long and feel so extreme.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Uranus's sideways spin is best explained by a giant collision that changed its rotation axis early in Solar System history.
lesson.deeperDive: Most models point to a giant impact as the reason Uranus is so tilted, although the details of the collision are still being refined. A large impactor could have transferred enough angular momentum to change the planet's spin without destroying it. Because Uranus does not have a large stabilizing moon like Earth's Moon, the tilt could persist for billions of years. The orientation has major consequences for weather and heat flow, and it helps explain why Uranus's seasons are so unusual compared with the other giant planets. Its methane-rich atmosphere also gives the planet its blue-green color, while its magnetic field remains oddly tilted and offset from the center.
lesson.funFact: A single season on Uranus lasts about 21 Earth years, so each pole spends a very long time in daylight or darkness.
references: Planetary science papers on the giant impact hypothesis
Hubble observations of Uranus seasonal change
